node_a:0:8b131250.text: Common symptoms of COVID-19 include cough, fever, and loss of smell and taste.

evidence_chunks.text: The symptoms of COVID-19 are variable depending on the type of variant contracted, ranging from mild symptoms to a potentially fatal illness.[41][42] Common symptoms include coughing, fever, loss of smell and taste, with less common ones including headaches, nasal congestion and runny nose, muscle pain, sore throat, diarrhea, eye irritation,[43] and toes swelling or turning purple,[44] and in moderate to severe cases, breathing difficulties.[45] People with the COVID-19 infection may have different symptoms, and their symptoms may change over time.

node_a:1:57b86ae8.text: Other symptoms may include fatigue, breathing difficulties, muscle pain, sore throat, and diarrhea.

evidence_chunks.text: The symptoms of COVID-19 are variable depending on the type of variant contracted, ranging from mild symptoms to a potentially fatal illness.[41][42] Common symptoms include coughing, fever, loss of smell and taste, with less common ones including headaches, nasal congestion and runny nose, muscle pain, sore throat, diarrhea, eye irritation,[43] and toes swelling or turning purple,[44] and in moderate to severe cases, breathing difficulties.[45] People with the COVID-19 infection may have different symptoms, and their symptoms may change over time.

node_a:2:b4473c89.text: Loss of taste and smell can occur in up to 88% of symptomatic cases without prior ear, nose, or throat disorders.

evidence_chunks.text: Three common clusters of symptoms have been identified: a respiratory symptom cluster with cough, sputum, shortness of breath, and fever; a musculoskeletal symptom cluster with muscle and joint pain, headache, and fatigue; and a cluster of digestive symptoms with abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea.[45] In people without prior ear, nose, or throat disorders, loss of taste combined with loss of smell is associated with COVID-19 and is reported in as many as 88% of symptomatic cases.[46][47][48]

node_a:3:d766d9b4.text: Symptoms can vary based on the variant and may change over time, ranging from mild to severe.

evidence_chunks.text: The symptoms of COVID-19 are variable depending on the type of variant contracted, ranging from mild symptoms to a potentially fatal illness.[41][42] Common symptoms include coughing, fever, loss of smell and taste, with less common ones including headaches, nasal congestion and runny nose, muscle pain, sore throat, diarrhea, eye irritation,[43] and toes swelling or turning purple,[44] and in moderate to severe cases, breathing difficulties.[45] People with the COVID-19 infection may have different symptoms, and their symptoms may change over time.

node_a:4:8edb4b71.text: In severe cases, symptoms may include shortness of breath and require hospitalization.

evidence_chunks.text: The severity of COVID‑19 varies. The disease may take a mild course with few or no symptoms, resembling other common upper respiratory diseases such as the common cold. In 3–4% of cases (7.4% for those over age 65) symptoms are severe enough to cause hospitalisation.[288] Mild cases typically recover within two weeks, while those with severe or critical diseases may take three to six weeks to recover

node_b:0:0de394cf.text: Common symptoms include dry cough, shortness of breath, fatigue, and loss of taste or smell.

evidence_chunks.text:
Create your personalized vaccination plan.
Symptoms

COVID-19 symptoms often show up 2 to 14 days after contact with the virus. You may have common cold symptoms, such as congestion, runny nose or sore throat.

Other symptoms include:

    Dry cough.
    Shortness of breath.
    Loss of taste or smell.
    Extreme tiredness, called fatigue.
    Digestive symptoms such as upset stomach, vomiting or loose stools, also called diarrhea.
    Pain, such as headaches and body or muscle aches.
    Fever or chills.

node_b:1:da908f2d.text: Other symptoms may include fever, chills, headaches, body aches, and digestive issues like upset stomach or diarrhea.

node_b:3:04f3a062.text: Severe symptoms requiring emergency help include trouble breathing, persistent chest pain, and new confusion.

evidence_chunks.text:
People who are at high risk of serious illness may get medicine to block the spread of the COVID-19 virus in the body. Or your healthcare team may plan regular checks to monitor your health.

Get emergency help right away for any of these symptoms in adults:

    Trouble breathing or catching your breath.
    Skin, lips or nail beds that are pale, gray or blue.
    New confusion.
    Trouble staying awake or waking up.
    Chest pain or pressure that is constant.

Get emergency help right away for any of those symptoms in children, as well as the following symptoms:

node_b:4:f1c95092.text: Some individuals may experience symptoms worsening around 5 to 7 days after onset.

evidence_chunks.text:
You may have only a few symptoms or none. People who have no symptoms but test positive for COVID-19 are called asymptomatic. For example, many children who test positive don't have symptoms of COVID-19 illness. People who go on to have symptoms are considered presymptomatic. People in both groups can still spread the COVID-19 virus to others.

Some people may have symptoms that get worse about 5 to 7 days after symptoms start.

node_c:0:0a2473ca.text: The most common symptoms of COVID-19 include fever, cough, and tiredness.

evidence_chunks.text:
The most common symptoms are fever, cough, and tiredness. Other symptoms can include shortness of breath, chest pain or pressure, muscle or body aches, headache, loss of taste or smell, confusion, sore throat, congestion or runny nose, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting, abdominal pain, and skin rashes. In addition to these symptoms, infants may have difficulty feeding.

Children of any age can become ill with COVID-19. While children and adults experience similar symptoms, children generally have less serious illness than adults.
